_meta.authoringNotes: Diagnostic criteria per EULAR/ACR 2017 (Lundberg PMID 29106200). Cancer association per Hill 2001 (PMID 11119988), Qiang 2017 (PMID 29045577). MDA5-ILD per Sato 2009 (PMID 19208611). IVIG ProDERM trial (Aggarwal 2022 PMID 36251879). Rituximab RIM trial (Oddis 2013 PMID 23044826). Epidemiology per Smoyer-Tomic 2012 (PMID 22573336).

h1Title: Dermatomyositis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Dermatomyositis is a rare autoimmune myopathy with characteristic cutaneous features, affecting roughly 1-10 new cases per million adults per year. It is defined by symmetric proximal muscle weakness developing over weeks to months alongside pathognomonic skin signs — heliotrope rash on the upper eyelids and Gottron papules over the knuckles, elbows, and knees. The disease has a bimodal age distribution, peaking in children aged 5-15 (juvenile dermatomyositis) and adults aged 40-60. Pulmonary involvement, oesophageal dysmotility, calcinosis, and an increased risk of underlying malignancy (3-7 times higher than the general population, especially in the first three years) dominate the prognosis. Modern treatment combines high-dose glucocorticoids, methotrexate or mycophenolate, intravenous immunoglobulin (ProDERM trial 2022), and rituximab or JAK inhibitors for refractory disease, with cancer surveillance an essential adjunct.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence 1-10 per million adults; prevalence approximately 10-20 per 100,000
keyStats.demographics: Female:male ratio approximately 2:1; juvenile form roughly equal sex distribution
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 200,000-300,000 people worldwide; cancer-associated dermatomyositis accounts for 15-25% of adult cases

definition: Dermatomyositis (ICD-10: M33.1, M33.0 for juvenile) is an immune-mediated inflammatory myopathy characterised by symmetric proximal muscle weakness and pathognomonic skin findings. The pathology centres on a perifascicular atrophy pattern with complement-mediated capillary injury and a Type 1 interferon signature; the disease is now understood as a humoral and interferon-driven vasculopathy rather than a purely T-cell-mediated myositis. The 2017 EULAR/ACR classification criteria require characteristic skin findings (heliotrope, Gottron) or muscle weakness pattern, supported by elevated muscle enzymes (creatine kinase, aldolase), abnormal electromyography, MRI muscle oedema, and biopsy showing perifascicular atrophy. Myositis-specific autoantibodies define clinically meaningful subsets: anti-Mi-2 (classic skin and muscle, good response), anti-MDA5 (clinically amyopathic with rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease, especially in East Asians), anti-TIF1γ and anti-NXP2 (strong cancer association in adults), anti-SAE (rapid skin onset with subsequent muscle disease), and anti-synthetase antibodies including Jo-1 (interstitial lung disease, mechanic's hands, Raynaud). The disease is managed by rheumatology with dermatology, neurology, pulmonology, and oncology input depending on phenotype.

typesClassification.description: Proximal muscle weakness plus characteristic cutaneous features (heliotrope rash, Gottron papules). Most adult cases; responds to glucocorticoids plus methotrexate.
Characteristic skin disease for at least 6 months without clinical or laboratory muscle involvement. Often anti-MDA5 antibody-positive; high risk of rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease.
Onset under age 16. Higher rates of calcinosis cutis and vasculopathy than adult disease; lower rates of associated cancer; better long-term prognosis with aggressive early treatment.

Underlying malignancy diagnosed within 3 years of dermatomyositis onset, occurring in 15-25% of adult cases. Strong association with anti-TIF1γ and anti-NXP2 antibodies; ovarian, lung, breast, gastric, colorectal, pancreatic, and nasopharyngeal cancers (in East Asia) most common.

Defined by anti-synthetase antibodies (anti-Jo-1 most common; also PL-7, PL-12, EJ, OJ, KS, Zo, Ha). Triad of myositis, interstitial lung disease, and arthritis with mechanic's hands and Raynaud phenomenon.

Dermatomyositis features with another systemic autoimmune disease (lupus, scleroderma, mixed connective tissue disease, rheumatoid arthritis). Treatment is tailored to the dominant disease and overlap features.

primarySymptoms: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness developing over weeks to months — difficulty rising from chairs, climbing stairs, lifting arms overhead, or washing hair.
Heliotrope rash: violaceous discolouration of the upper eyelids with or without periorbital oedema, often the first cutaneous finding.
Gottron papules: pink-purple papules over the dorsal metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints; sometimes also elbows, knees, or medial malleoli.
Photodistributed violaceous erythema on the upper chest (V-sign), upper back and shoulders (shawl sign), and lateral thighs (holster sign).
Periungual erythema, dilated capillaries on capillaroscopy, and ragged cuticles with painful infarcts.
Mechanic's hands — fissured, hyperkeratotic, dry skin on the lateral fingers and palms — in the antisynthetase syndrome.
Dysphagia with choking or nasal regurgitation from pharyngeal and upper oesophageal striated muscle involvement.
Dyspnoea on exertion, dry cough, and reduced exercise tolerance from interstitial lung disease, particularly in anti-MDA5 and antisynthetase patients.
Polyarthralgia or non-erosive polyarthritis, usually small joints of the hands.
Calcinosis cutis — subcutaneous and intramuscular calcium deposits, most common in juvenile dermatomyositis.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent violaceous discolouration of the upper eyelids without an obvious cause
Photosensitive rash in a sun-exposed distribution (V-sign, shawl sign) developing over weeks
Difficulty climbing stairs or rising from low chairs without using the hands
Unexplained elevation of creatine kinase or transaminases on routine blood tests
New cough, breathlessness, or fall in exercise tolerance in a patient with characteristic skin signs
emergencySigns: Acute breathlessness, hypoxia, and bilateral pulmonary infiltrates — rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease, especially in anti-MDA5 patients
Severe dysphagia with aspiration pneumonia, weight loss, and inability to swallow saliva
Acute weakness affecting the diaphragm or respiratory muscles with falling vital capacity
Severe gastrointestinal vasculopathy with abdominal pain, melena, or perforation (juvenile dermatomyositis)
Newly identified malignancy presenting with weight loss, masses, or paraneoplastic features

causes.description: Upregulation of type I interferon (IFN-α/β) genes drives endothelial injury, complement deposition on capillaries, and perifascicular atrophy of muscle fibres. The interferon signature is now considered the unifying pathogenic feature.

Antibodies against intracellular antigens (Mi-2, MDA5, TIF1γ, NXP2, SAE, synthetases) define distinct clinical subsets and likely participate in pathogenesis. Each antibody is linked to a characteristic skin, muscle, lung, and cancer phenotype.

15-25% of adult cases have an underlying cancer diagnosed within 3 years of dermatomyositis onset. Strong association with anti-TIF1γ (risk roughly 50% in older adults) and anti-NXP2 antibodies. Tumour antigen-driven autoimmunity is the leading hypothesis.
HLA-DRB1*0301 (anti-Jo-1, anti-PM-Scl) and HLA-DRB1*0701 (anti-Mi-2) are well-established risk alleles. Genome-wide studies confirm multiple immune-regulatory loci.

Ultraviolet light exposure increases disease activity. Viral infections (coxsackie, parvovirus, HTLV-1, hepatitis C, SARS-CoV-2) have been implicated as triggers in case reports and cohort studies, particularly for juvenile disease.
Hydroxyurea, statins, anti-TNF agents, penicillamine, and immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1 and anti-PD-L1) can produce dermatomyositis-like illness, sometimes overlapping with classic disease.

riskFactors.description: Approximately 2:1 female predominance in adults. Juvenile dermatomyositis is roughly equal between sexes.
Bimodal age distribution. Cancer risk rises sharply with adult onset, particularly above age 60.
HLA-DRB1*0301 and HLA-DRB1*0701 are associated with antisynthetase and anti-Mi-2 subsets respectively. Genetic risk is small in individual terms but informs the immunological framework.
Cancer risk is highest in the first 3 years after dermatomyositis diagnosis (standardised incidence ratio 3-7) and particularly with anti-TIF1γ and anti-NXP2 antibodies.
Anti-MDA5 antibodies and rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease are more common in East Asian patients (Japanese, Chinese, Korean cohorts) than in Western populations.
UV light precipitates and worsens cutaneous disease and may have a role in inducing autoimmunity. Strict photoprotection is recommended.

riskFactors.factor: Use of certain drugs (statins, hydroxyurea, checkpoint inhibitors)
riskFactors.description: Can induce dermatomyositis-like syndromes. Drug history is essential at diagnosis.

affectedDemographics: Adult dermatomyositis: annual incidence 5-10 per million in Western populations (Smoyer-Tomic 2012); prevalence around 1-2 per 10,000.
Juvenile dermatomyositis: 2-4 new cases per million children per year; the most common idiopathic inflammatory myopathy in childhood.
East Asia: higher prevalence of anti-MDA5 antibodies and rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease.
Cancer-associated dermatomyositis: 15-25% of adult cases; ovarian, lung, breast, gastric, colorectal, pancreatic, and nasopharyngeal cancers predominate.
Female predominance is 2:1 in adults; equal sex distribution in childhood disease.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis follows the 2017 EULAR/ACR classification criteria, which use a probability score based on age at onset, pattern of muscle weakness, cutaneous features, laboratory findings, and (where available) muscle biopsy. The minimum workup includes serum creatine kinase, aldolase, transaminases (often raised from muscle), lactate dehydrogenase, full blood count, urinalysis, antinuclear antibody, and myositis-specific antibody panel covering Mi-2, MDA5, TIF1γ, NXP2, SAE, and the synthetases (Jo-1 plus less common targets). Electromyography typically shows short-duration low-amplitude motor unit potentials with fibrillations and complex repetitive discharges; MRI of the thighs demonstrates muscle oedema (STIR hyperintensity) and atrophy and guides biopsy site. Muscle biopsy showing perifascicular atrophy, perivascular CD4 and B-cell infiltrates, MHC class I upregulation, and capillary depletion is highly supportive. High-resolution CT chest, pulmonary function tests with DLCO, and echocardiography assess interstitial lung disease and cardiac involvement. Video-fluoroscopic swallow study identifies pharyngeal and oesophageal dysphagia. All adults must undergo malignancy screening — chest, abdominal, and pelvic CT, mammography, gynaecological examination, faecal occult blood or colonoscopy if indicated by age, and tumour markers (CA-125 in women, PSA in men) — repeated annually for 3 years from diagnosis, given the 15-25% cancer association.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum creatine kinase, aldolase, and transaminases
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies muscle damage; elevations 5-50 times the upper limit of normal are typical of active myositis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, results same day. Repeated every 2-4 weeks to monitor treatment response.

diagnosticTests.test: Myositis-specific autoantibody panel (Mi-2, MDA5, TIF1γ, NXP2, SAE, Jo-1 and other synthetases)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines clinical subset, predicts cancer risk, lung disease, and treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single blood draw; results return within 1-2 weeks. Anti-MDA5 testing is particularly important in clinically amyopathic patients.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms inflammatory myopathic pattern with short-duration low-amplitude motor units, fibrillations, and complex repetitive discharges
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A neurophysiologist inserts a thin needle electrode into several muscles; mild brief discomfort. 30-45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of thigh and proximal limb muscles (T2/STIR)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects muscle oedema and fatty replacement; guides muscle biopsy site and monitors treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies still in the MRI scanner for 30-45 minutes. No radiation. Report within 1-2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis through perifascicular atrophy, complement deposition, and capillary depletion
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Day-case open or needle biopsy under local anaesthetic from quadriceps or deltoid. Small wound healed within 2 weeks. Histology reported within 7-14 days.

diagnosticTests.test: High-resolution CT chest and pulmonary function tests with DLCO
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects interstitial lung disease, the major cause of mortality in dermatomyositis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT scan takes 5-10 minutes; pulmonary function tests 30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Cancer screening (CT chest-abdomen-pelvis, mammography, gynaecological exam, age-appropriate colonoscopy, tumour markers)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects underlying malignancy in 15-25% of adult patients; repeated annually for 3 years
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Coordinated workup over 1-2 weeks; specific tests depend on age, sex, and antibody profile.

treatmentOverview: Treatment combines glucocorticoids with steroid-sparing immunosuppressants to control skin and muscle disease, plus organ-specific therapy for interstitial lung disease, dysphagia, and calcinosis. Initial therapy is oral prednisolone 0.75-1 mg/kg/day (max 60-80 mg) with rapid step-down once muscle enzymes normalise; pulse intravenous methylprednisolone 500-1,000 mg for 3 days is used for severe muscle weakness, dysphagia, or rapidly progressive lung disease. Methotrexate (15-25 mg weekly) or mycophenolate mofetil (2-3 g/day) is started in the first 4-8 weeks to allow steroid tapering; azathioprine is an alternative. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg over 2-5 days every 4 weeks is now a first-line steroid-sparing therapy following the ProDERM phase 3 trial (Aggarwal NEJM 2022) which demonstrated significant improvement in 79% of IVIG-treated versus 44% placebo patients at 16 weeks. Refractory disease responds to rituximab (1 g day 0 and day 14), with 83% of patients meeting improvement criteria in the RIM study at 44 weeks. JAK inhibitors (tofacitinib 5 mg twice daily, baricitinib) target the type 1 interferon pathway and are increasingly used in refractory dermatomyositis with skin or lung dominant disease. Anti-MDA5 rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease has high mortality and is treated with intensive triple therapy: high-dose glucocorticoids, intravenous cyclophosphamide or tacrolimus, plus tofacitinib or rituximab. Hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day controls cutaneous disease; strict photoprotection with SPF 50+ sunscreen is essential. Calcinosis is difficult to treat but may respond to diltiazem, bisphosphonates, sodium thiosulphate, or surgical excision. Cancer screening (CT, mammography, colonoscopy, gynaecological examination, tumour markers) is repeated annually for 3 years. Treatment of underlying malignancy often improves the dermatomyositis substantially.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisolone 0.75-1 mg/kg/day (max 60-80 mg) tapered over months
medicalTreatments.description: Backbone induction therapy. Tapered as muscle enzymes normalise and clinical response is achieved; aim for 5-10 mg/day by 6-12 months. Add bone protection with calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-80% achieve significant muscle strength recovery within 3-6 months; long-term steroid toxicity drives the need for steroid-sparing agents

medicalTreatments.name: Pulse intravenous methylprednisolone (500-1,000 mg daily for 3 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used at induction for severe weakness, dysphagia, cardiac involvement, or rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid improvement within days; reduces total cumulative oral steroid dose

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate 15-25 mg weekly (oral or subcutaneous) plus folic acid
medicalTreatments.description: First-line steroid-sparing immunosuppressant. Start at 7.5-10 mg and titrate by 2.5 mg every 2 weeks. Monitor full blood count, liver enzymes, and renal function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Allows 30-50% reduction in steroid dose at 6 months; non-inferior to azathioprine in head-to-head trials

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil 2-3 g/day in divided doses
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative or add-on to methotrexate, especially with interstitial lung disease or hepatic dysfunction. Avoid in pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Similar steroid-sparing effect to methotrexate; preferred when ILD is prominent

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg over 2-5 days, every 4 weeks for 5 cycles
medicalTreatments.description: First-line steroid-sparing therapy following the ProDERM trial. Indicated for adult dermatomyositis with persistent muscle and skin disease, dysphagia, or steroid intolerance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ProDERM 2022: 79% improvement at 16 weeks versus 44% with placebo; sustained benefit in 71% at 40 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab 1 g intravenously day 0 and day 14, repeated every 6-12 months
medicalTreatments.description: B-cell depletion therapy for refractory or relapsing disease. Particularly effective in antisynthetase syndrome with interstitial lung disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: RIM trial: 83% achieved improvement criteria at 44 weeks; significantly improves lung function in antisynthetase ILD

medicalTreatments.name: JAK inhibitor (tofacitinib 5 mg twice daily, baricitinib 4 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Targets the type 1 interferon signalling pathway. Used off-label for refractory cutaneous, muscle, or lung disease, including anti-MDA5 ILD.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Promising in open-label series with 60-70% improvement in refractory cutaneous and lung dermatomyositis; randomised trials ongoing

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day (max 400 mg)
medicalTreatments.description: Useful for cutaneous disease, especially photodistributed rashes. Add annual ophthalmology screening after 5 years for retinopathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest cutaneous benefit in 40-60% of patients; rarely sufficient as monotherapy

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical excision of large symptomatic calcinosis deposits
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for calcinosis causing chronic ulceration, secondary infection, or functional impairment. Recurrence after excision is common; perioperative immunosuppression aims to reduce regrowth.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic improvement in 60-70% of patients; recurrence in adjacent sites 30-40%

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) feeding
surgicalOptions.description: Used in severe dysphagia with aspiration risk despite immunosuppression. Provides safe nutrition during recovery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Nutritional support adequate in over 90%; some patients reverse to oral intake as myositis improves

alternativeTreatments.name: Strict photoprotection and topical therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Daily SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen, sun-protective clothing, and avoidance of midday sun. Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment or potent corticosteroids for localised facial and hand cutaneous disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physical therapy and graded exercise programme
alternativeTreatments.description: Once muscle inflammation is controlled, progressive resistance and aerobic training improve strength and function. Early mobilisation during acute disease is also beneficial — bed rest worsens deconditioning.

whySeeSpecialist: Suspected dermatomyositis requires rheumatology referral for myositis-specific antibody testing, EMG, MRI, biopsy, and ILD workup. Anti-MDA5 patients with rapidly progressive lung disease should be managed jointly with respiratory medicine in a tertiary centre. Cancer screening should be coordinated with oncology and primary care. Juvenile cases need paediatric rheumatology with calcinosis and growth surveillance.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatology centre with access to the full myositis antibody panel, muscle biopsy and pathology expertise, and a multidisciplinary ILD service. Ask about IVIG, rituximab, and JAK inhibitor availability. Confirm a structured cancer screening pathway for adult patients.
hospitalCriteria: Multidisciplinary myositis clinic (rheumatology, neurology, dermatology, pulmonology)
Access to full myositis-specific antibody panel
Muscle biopsy and renal/muscle pathology service
High-resolution CT chest and lung function testing
Cancer screening pathway and oncology liaison
keyFacilities: Tertiary rheumatology centres with myositis subspecialty clinics
Specialist ILD and pulmonary fibrosis services
Oncology and gynaecology services for cancer screening
preventionStrategies: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen daily and reapply during outdoor activity to limit cutaneous flares.
Avoid midday sun exposure and use UV-protective clothing and wide-brimmed hats.
Review all current medications for known dermatomyositis triggers (statins, hydroxyurea, checkpoint inhibitors) at diagnosis.
Adhere strictly to immunosuppressive therapy to maintain remission and reduce calcinosis risk.
Attend cancer screening at diagnosis and annually for 3 years.
Vaccinate against influenza, pneumococcus, SARS-CoV-2, HPV, and shingles before high-dose immunosuppression.
lifestyleModifications: Use SPF 50+ sunscreen and protective clothing every day.
Adhere to immunosuppressive medications as prescribed; do not stop suddenly.
Engage in supervised physical therapy and graded exercise to maintain strength.
Maintain a balanced anti-inflammatory diet with adequate protein and calcium.
Stop smoking and limit alcohol to under 14 units per week.
Attend regular cancer screening visits during the first 3 years after diagnosis.

recommended: Mediterranean-style diet rich in fish, olive oil, vegetables, and fruits
Adequate protein intake (1.2-1.6 g/kg/day) during muscle recovery
Vitamin D and calcium supplementation to offset steroid-induced bone loss
Soft-texture foods and thickened liquids if pharyngeal dysphagia is present
avoid: Excess simple sugars and refined carbohydrates that worsen steroid-induced metabolic syndrome
Heavy alcohol intake which exacerbates fatty liver and bone loss
Raw or undercooked foods during high-dose immunosuppression (Listeria risk)
Grapefruit and grapefruit juice while on tacrolimus or other calcineurin inhibitors

exerciseGuidelines: Begin gentle range-of-motion exercises and light isometric work during the acute inflammatory phase under physiotherapy guidance — bed rest worsens deconditioning. Once muscle enzymes normalise, progress to graded resistance training and aerobic exercise (3-5 sessions per week, 30-45 minutes). Severe weakness or interstitial lung disease may need pulmonary rehabilitation. Avoid sun exposure during outdoor exercise.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed prednisolone, methotrexate, or mycophenolate at the same time each day.
Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen every morning and reapply during outdoor time.
Attend infusion appointments for IVIG, rituximab, or cyclophosphamide as scheduled.
Monitor for new rash, weakness, dysphagia, or breathlessness and report promptly.
Perform prescribed physical therapy exercises twice daily during recovery.
Attend regular blood tests for muscle enzymes, full blood count, liver and renal function.
prognosis: Outlook has improved substantially over the past three decades. With modern combination therapy, 5-year survival is now 80-90% in adult dermatomyositis without ILD or cancer, and 95% in juvenile disease. The main predictors of poor outcome are anti-MDA5 antibody with rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease (1-year mortality up to 40-60% in untreated East Asian cohorts), associated malignancy, severe dysphagia with aspiration, and cardiac involvement. Anti-TIF1γ-positive adult patients have the highest cancer risk (around 50% within 3 years). Sustained remission off all immunosuppression is achieved by a minority; most patients require long-term low-dose steroids or steroid-sparing therapy. Calcinosis develops in 30-70% of juvenile cases and 10-20% of adults and is difficult to reverse. Treatment of an associated cancer often substantially improves dermatomyositis; persistent disease after cancer therapy implies a separate non-paraneoplastic course.
recoveryTimeline: Muscle enzymes typically fall within 4-8 weeks of induction. Strength recovery lags enzyme normalisation by 6-12 weeks. Cutaneous improvement is often slower than muscle improvement. Maximum response usually occurs by 6-12 months. Steroid tapering targets prednisolone 5-10 mg/day by 6-12 months. Long-term maintenance on methotrexate or mycophenolate is typical for 2-5 years.
complications: Interstitial lung disease, especially anti-MDA5 rapidly progressive form — major cause of mortality; screen all patients with HRCT and pulmonary function tests.
Associated malignancy in 15-25% of adult cases — repeat cancer screening annually for 3 years from diagnosis.
Calcinosis cutis with chronic ulceration and infection, common in juvenile disease and refractory to most therapies.
Severe dysphagia with aspiration pneumonia and weight loss.
Cardiac involvement (myocarditis, conduction disease, heart failure) — screen with ECG and echocardiography at diagnosis and during flares.
Treatment-related complications: osteoporosis, diabetes, cataracts, opportunistic infection, secondary malignancy.

supportResources.name: The Myositis Association
supportResources.description: US-based patient organisation for myositis (including dermatomyositis) with peer support, research updates, and disease information.

supportResources.name: Myositis UK
supportResources.description: UK patient charity offering helpline, regional groups, and patient information for myositis and dermatomyositis.

supportResources.name: Cure JM Foundation (Juvenile Myositis)
supportResources.description: International nonprofit focused on juvenile dermatomyositis with family support and research funding.

supportResources.name: European League Against Rheumatism (EULAR)
supportResources.description: European specialty society with patient-focused information and current treatment recommendations.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus produces a malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds, photosensitive skin, polyarthritis, and serositis with positive ANA and anti-dsDNA. Dermatomyositis has heliotrope rash on the eyelids, Gottron papules over the knuckles, and proximal muscle weakness with elevated creatine kinase; muscle biopsy and myositis-specific antibodies distinguish it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Polymyositis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Polymyositis shares proximal muscle weakness but lacks the characteristic cutaneous features of dermatomyositis. Biopsy shows endomysial CD8 T-cell infiltrate around non-necrotic fibres rather than perifascicular atrophy. Cancer association is weaker than in dermatomyositis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Psoriasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Psoriasis produces well-demarcated salmon-coloured scaly plaques on extensor surfaces but no proximal muscle weakness, heliotrope rash, or Gottron papules. Skin biopsy and absence of myositis-specific antibodies distinguish it.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Scleroderma features skin thickening, Raynaud phenomenon, telangiectasia, and characteristic anti-centromere or anti-Scl-70 antibodies. Dermatomyositis has predominant muscle weakness with violaceous rash; overlap syndromes occur and share interstitial lung disease.

relatedConditions.name: Polymyositis
relatedConditions.relevance: Closely related idiopathic inflammatory myopathy without the characteristic skin disease; shared treatment approach.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus
relatedConditions.relevance: Major differential and frequent overlap syndrome; both share photosensitive rash and interstitial lung disease risk.

relatedConditions.name: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
relatedConditions.relevance: Overlap with dermatomyositis (anti-PM-Scl, scleromyositis) is well described; shared management of ILD.

relatedConditions.name: Raynaud Phenomenon
relatedConditions.relevance: Common feature of antisynthetase syndrome; cold-induced vasospasm of the fingers.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Polyarthritis is a frequent feature of antisynthetase syndrome and an important differential.

faqs.question: What is dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Dermatomyositis is a rare autoimmune disease causing inflammation of muscle and skin. Patients develop symmetric proximal muscle weakness, a violaceous rash on the eyelids (heliotrope), and Gottron papules over the knuckles, elbows, and knees. It can also affect the lungs, heart, and oesophagus.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Early signs include a violaceous discolouration of the upper eyelids, a photosensitive rash over the chest and shoulders, and progressive difficulty climbing stairs, rising from chairs, or lifting arms above the head. Skin signs often precede muscle symptoms by weeks to months.

faqs.question: Is dermatomyositis associated with cancer?
faqs.answer: Yes. About 15-25% of adult cases have an underlying malignancy diagnosed within 3 years of dermatomyositis. Anti-TIF1-gamma antibody is the strongest predictor. Annual cancer screening (CT, mammography, gynaecological exam, colonoscopy, tumour markers) is recommended for 3 years after diagnosis.

faqs.question: How is dermatomyositis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis uses the 2017 EULAR/ACR criteria combining characteristic skin signs, proximal muscle weakness, elevated muscle enzymes, abnormal EMG, MRI muscle oedema, and biopsy showing perifascicular atrophy. Myositis-specific antibodies (Mi-2, MDA5, TIF1-gamma, NXP2, SAE, synthetases) define the clinical subset.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is oral prednisolone 0.75-1 mg/kg/day combined with methotrexate or mycophenolate mofetil. Intravenous immunoglobulin is now widely used as a steroid-sparing agent based on the ProDERM trial. Rituximab and JAK inhibitors are reserved for refractory or severe disease.

faqs.question: Is dermatomyositis curable?
faqs.answer: Dermatomyositis is not curable but can be brought into long remission with treatment. About 60-80% of adults achieve substantial improvement on combination immunosuppression. Most patients need long-term low-dose maintenance therapy. Cancer-associated cases often improve when the underlying tumour is treated.

faqs.question: What is anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis is a clinically amyopathic subtype with little muscle disease but a high risk of rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease, especially in East Asian patients. It carries 1-year mortality up to 40-60% without intensive triple therapy combining steroids, calcineurin inhibitors, and JAK inhibitors or rituximab.

faqs.question: What is the heliotrope rash?
faqs.answer: Heliotrope rash is a violaceous (lilac-coloured) discolouration of the upper eyelids, sometimes with periorbital swelling. It is named after the colour of the heliotrope flower. It is pathognomonic for dermatomyositis when seen with proximal muscle weakness or Gottron papules.

faqs.question: What are Gottron papules?
faqs.answer: Gottron papules are pink-purple, flat-topped papules over the dorsal metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints, and sometimes elbows, knees, or medial malleoli. They are pathognomonic for dermatomyositis and often accompany the heliotrope rash and proximal muscle weakness.

faqs.question: Can children get dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Juvenile dermatomyositis is the most common idiopathic inflammatory myopathy in childhood, with peak onset at age 5-15 years. Cancer association is much lower than in adults. Calcinosis cutis is more common but long-term outcomes are generally favourable with early aggressive treatment.

faqs.question: How does IVIG help dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Intravenous immunoglobulin given as 2 g/kg every 4 weeks improves muscle strength, skin disease, and dysphagia. The ProDERM phase 3 trial (NEJM 2022) showed significant improvement in 79% of IVIG patients versus 44% on placebo at 16 weeks, with benefit sustained at 40 weeks.

faqs.question: Is sunlight a trigger for dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Ultraviolet light exposure precipitates and worsens cutaneous disease. Patients should use SPF 50+ broad-spectrum sunscreen daily, UV-protective clothing, and avoid midday sun. Photoprotection often reduces the need for higher-dose immunosuppression.

faqs.question: Can dermatomyositis affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Interstitial lung disease affects 20-40% of adult dermatomyositis patients and is the main cause of mortality. Anti-MDA5 antibody predicts rapidly progressive ILD; anti-synthetase antibodies predict chronic ILD. High-resolution CT and pulmonary function tests are essential at diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is exercise safe with dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Once acute inflammation is controlled, supervised aerobic and resistance exercise improves strength, function, and quality of life. Gentle range-of-motion exercises during the acute phase prevent contractures and deconditioning. Avoid sun exposure during outdoor exercise.

faqs.question: What is calcinosis cutis?
faqs.answer: Calcinosis cutis is calcium deposition in skin, subcutaneous tissue, and sometimes muscle, leading to firm nodules, chronic ulcers, and secondary infection. It affects 30-70% of juvenile and 10-20% of adult cases. Treatments include diltiazem, bisphosphonates, sodium thiosulphate, and surgical excision for severe cases.

faqs.question: Are statins safe in dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Statins can rarely trigger a dermatomyositis-like syndrome or worsen pre-existing disease. They should be reviewed at diagnosis and stopped if a temporal association is suspected. Hyperlipidaemia from steroid therapy may still need treatment with alternative agents.

faqs.question: Can dermatomyositis come back after remission?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relapses occur in 30-50% of patients within 5 years, often when immunosuppression is reduced. Monitoring muscle enzymes, skin signs, and myositis-specific antibody titres detects relapse early. Restarting or escalating immunosuppression usually regains control.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Five-year survival in adult dermatomyositis without ILD or cancer is now 80-90%, and 95% in juvenile disease. Anti-MDA5 with rapidly progressive ILD and cancer-associated disease have substantially worse prognosis. Cardiovascular disease, infection, and malignancy are leading competing risks long-term.

faqs.question: Is dermatomyositis hereditary?
faqs.answer: There is a genetic susceptibility component (HLA-DRB1*0301, HLA-DRB1*0701, and several immune-regulatory loci), but the disease is not directly inherited. Most cases are sporadic. Family members rarely develop dermatomyositis themselves but may have other autoimmune conditions.

faqs.question: Can I get pregnant with dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Yes, when the disease is in remission for at least 6 months on pregnancy-safe medications (hydroxychloroquine, azathioprine, low-dose steroids, IVIG). Mycophenolate and methotrexate are teratogenic and must be stopped 3 months before conception. Multidisciplinary care improves outcomes.

faqs.question: How often should I have cancer screening?
faqs.answer: All adults with new dermatomyositis should have age-appropriate cancer screening at diagnosis — chest, abdominal, and pelvic CT, mammography, gynaecological exam, colonoscopy if indicated, and tumour markers. Repeat screening every 6-12 months for at least 3 years, when cancer risk is highest.

metaDescription: Dermatomyositis: heliotrope rash, Gottron papules, muscle weakness, and cancer link. Diagnosis, IVIG, rituximab, JAK inhibitors, and prognosis explained.
